name: mushroom bourguignon
notes:
Vegetarian riff on boeuf bourguignon — mushrooms and vegan beef stand in for the beef.

TECHNIQUE: start with sauteed mushrooms in butter, then flour for roux. Other vegetables like celery/carrot are stew vegetables coarsely chopped, not sauce base (finely chopped and sauteed).

VEGAN BEEF: use a very neutral vegan ground beef — Abbott's ground beef is probably the best. Seitan is not appropriate; impossible meat is borderline (too distinctly flavored). Flavor in the vegetarian version is NOT derived from the beef, so a good vegan beef bouillon is doing the heavy lifting.

HERBS / AROMATICS (non-optional): rosemary and bay leaf in large quantities are mandatory. Rosemary is nice-to-have in chicken stews or italian vegetable soups, but it's mandatory in this vegetarian beef stew — the wine + soy meat + potatoes + mushrooms + carrots will be flat without the sharpness of bay leaf and rosemary together. Garlic is also non-optional. Use sharp white onions, not yellow or red.

NORTHERN PULL: worcestershire sauce rounds it out and pushes the flavor more 'northern'. Without it the rosemary/bay/tomato combination can drift italian even with the potatoes.

TOMATO: use more tomato paste than traditional boeuf bourguignon, and add a chopped fresh tomato as extra tomato flavor — you get less umami from the pseudo meat than from real beef, so compensate here. Tomatoes are non-optional for this recipe.

RED CHILI FLAKES: a real ingredient in this dish, not a topping. The chili flakes pair particularly well with the tomato + worcestershire (or tamarind paste) axis — they activate the tang and amplify the depth. Without them the dish reads slightly flatter against the same ingredient set. Stir into the simmer; don't sprinkle at the end.

IMPOSSIBLE / BEYOND BEEF — COOK AS MEATBALLS, NOT CRUMBLES: if using impossible meat, beyond beef, or similar extruded plant-based ground beef, form it into MEATBALLS before adding to the stew. Don't break it into crumbles. The extruded proteins in these products recombine during baking — crumbles turn back into a monolithic slab of pseudo-meat in the pot. Pre-formed meatballs hold their shape, and the dish ends up with discrete chunks instead of one giant brick. This is unique to the soy-based products; real ground beef and abbott's-style products don't behave this way.
